stem: You are the GP registrar reviewing a 7-year-old boy with asthma at his routine 3-monthly clinic visit. He currently uses inhaled budesonide 100 micrograms twice daily with spacer and salbutamol as required. Over the past 3 months he has needed his salbutamol 3 to 4 times a week, woken at night with cough twice, and missed 2 days of school for an exacerbation managed by oral prednisolone. His inhaler technique is excellent on demonstration. There is no environmental trigger he can identify and no smoke exposure at home.

data_table.Examination: Chest clear today; no wheeze; growth on 50th centile; no eczema flare
data_table.Investigations: Spirometry FEV1 78% predicted, FEV1/FVC 0.78, bronchodilator response +14%
data_table.Medications: Budesonide 100 mcg BD with spacer; salbutamol 100 mcg PRN; nil oral

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Increase inhaled budesonide to 200 micrograms twice daily; reinforce trigger avoidance and review in 6 weeks
options.rationale: Poor control on low-dose ICS in a child aged 6 to 11 with good technique warrants step-up to medium-dose ICS as the preferred next step before combination therapy. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook)

options.text: Add montelukast 5 mg orally each evening; continue current inhaled budesonide and salbutamol
options.rationale: Montelukast is an add-on option but is less effective than increasing inhaled corticosteroid dose or adding a long-acting beta-agonist in this age group. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook)

options.text: Switch budesonide to inhaled fluticasone/salmeterol combination 50/25 micrograms twice daily for combined therapy
options.rationale: Combination ICS/LABA is reserved for children aged 6 to 11 only after medium-dose ICS has failed; jumping directly from low-dose ICS to ICS/LABA is not the recommended step. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook)

options.text: Cease budesonide and start oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily for 14 days; review at completion of course
options.rationale: Maintenance oral corticosteroids carry growth and adrenal suppression risk and are reserved for severe refractory asthma after specialist review, not for routine step-up. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook)

options.text: Continue current regimen and provide written action plan; review only if further exacerbation requires hospital
options.rationale: Three to four reliever uses weekly, nocturnal cough and a recent steroid-requiring exacerbation define poorly controlled asthma; continuing without escalation accepts ongoing morbidity. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook)

explanation.summary: Asthma control in children aged 6 to 11 is assessed over the previous 4 weeks: daytime symptoms more than twice a week, reliever use more than twice a week, any nocturnal waking, or any activity limitation indicates poor control. Step-up from low-dose ICS in this age group is to medium-dose ICS as the preferred option, with montelukast or LABA combinations reserved for failure of medium-dose ICS. Technique, adherence, trigger exposure and alternative diagnoses must be checked at every step.
key_points: Children 6 to 11 step-up: low-dose ICS to medium-dose ICS first
ICS/LABA combination reserved for failure of medium-dose ICS in this age group
Reassess control, technique, adherence and triggers at every step
Document a written action plan at every escalation
explanation.pearls: Budesonide 200 micrograms twice daily is the medium-dose threshold for children 6 to 11; fluticasone propionate equivalent is 200 micrograms twice daily.

stem: You are the GP registrar reviewing a 22-month-old boy whose mother is worried that he looks pale and tires easily on outings. He drinks 800 mL of cow's milk daily from a bottle since 9 months of age and eats little solid food, refusing meat and vegetables. He was born at term, breastfed for 4 months. He is otherwise well, with no jaundice, splenomegaly or recent infection. His growth has tracked the 25th centile. Bloods returned this morning.

data_table.Examination: Pale conjunctivae; no jaundice; soft abdomen, no organomegaly; no lymphadenopathy
data_table.Investigations: Hb 78, MCV 62, MCH 19, ferritin 7 microg/L, transferrin saturation 7%, CRP 2, reticulocytes 1.2%

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Reduce cow's milk to under 500 mL/day and start oral ferrous sulfate 3 mg elemental iron/kg daily; recheck in 1 month
options.rationale: Iron-deficiency anaemia in a toddler with excess cow's milk intake requires both dietary modification and elemental iron at 3 to 6 mg/kg/day; recheck FBC in 4 to 6 weeks to confirm response. (Source: RCH CPG: Iron deficiency)

options.text: Refer to paediatric haematology for bone marrow examination; defer iron therapy pending specialist review
options.rationale: Classic microcytic hypochromic anaemia with low ferritin and risk factors for iron deficiency does not require bone marrow examination; empirical iron with diet change and recheck is first line. (Source: RCH CPG: Iron deficiency)

options.text: Arrange same-day intravenous iron polymaltose infusion under sedation; admit for observation overnight
options.rationale: Intravenous iron is reserved for malabsorption, intolerance or non-adherence to oral iron after a trial; first-line is oral iron with dietary advice. (Source: RCH CPG: Iron deficiency)

options.text: Transfuse packed red cells 10 mL/kg over 4 hours; admit for paediatric review and post-transfusion bloods
options.rationale: Transfusion is reserved for symptomatic anaemia with haemodynamic compromise or Hb under 60 to 70 g/L in a sick child; this stable toddler does not meet criteria. (Source: RCH CPG: Iron deficiency)

options.text: Send haemoglobin electrophoresis to exclude thalassaemia trait; defer iron therapy until results return
options.rationale: Haemoglobin electrophoresis is unreliable during iron deficiency and is deferred until iron repletion; ferritin under 12 with low transferrin saturation is diagnostic. (Source: RCH CPG: Iron deficiency)

explanation.summary: Iron-deficiency anaemia in toddlers most commonly reflects excess cow's milk intake (which inhibits iron absorption and causes occult GI blood loss) and inadequate iron-rich solids. Workup shows microcytic, hypochromic anaemia with low ferritin and transferrin saturation. First-line management is dietary advice (cow's milk under 500 mL/day, iron-rich solids including red meat or fortified cereals) plus oral elemental iron 3 to 6 mg/kg/day. Reassess Hb at 4 to 6 weeks; expect a 10 g/L rise.
key_points: Toddler iron deficiency: limit cow's milk to under 500 mL/day
Oral ferrous sulfate 3 to 6 mg elemental iron/kg/day first line
Expect 10 g/L Hb rise at 4 weeks of treatment
Defer haemoglobin electrophoresis until iron-replete
explanation.pearls: Continue oral iron for 3 months after Hb normalises to replete stores; ferritin should rise above 30 microg/L.

stem: You are the paediatric registrar reviewing a 6-week-old infant in ED with 1 week of progressive paroxysmal cough, vomiting after coughing fits, and an apnoeic episode at home today. There is no fever. His 4-year-old sister has had a 3-week persistent cough recently labelled as a viral URTI. The infant has had no vaccinations yet; the family declined antenatal pertussis vaccination. Nasopharyngeal swab PCR for Bordetella pertussis is positive.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8, HR 168, RR 56, SpO2 92% RA (recovers between paroxysms), BP 78/46
data_table.Examination: Paroxysmal cough with inspiratory whoop and post-tussive vomit observed; chest otherwise clear
data_table.Investigations: Pertussis PCR positive; WCC 28 (lymphocyte predominant 18); CXR mild perihilar streaking

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management of the infant and household contacts?

options.text: Treat the infant with oral trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole and reserve chemoprophylaxis for symptomatic household contacts only
options.rationale: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole is contraindicated under 2 months due to kernicterus risk; symptom-based prophylaxis misses the asymptomatic infectious period. (Source: Australian Immunisation Handbook)

options.text: Treat the infant with oral amoxicillin and offer prophylaxis only to household contacts under 6 months of age
options.rationale: Amoxicillin has no role in pertussis; macrolides are first-line, and chemoprophylaxis extends to all close household contacts not just infants. (Source: Australian Immunisation Handbook)

options.text: Manage the infant supportively without antibiotics; arrange vaccination of household contacts but no chemoprophylaxis
options.rationale: Antibiotics reduce infectivity even when started late and prevent ongoing transmission; vaccination alone is too slow to protect contacts within the incubation window. (Source: Australian Immunisation Handbook)

options.text: Treat the infant with oral azithromycin and arrange azithromycin chemoprophylaxis for all close household contacts regardless of vaccination status
options.rationale: Azithromycin is first-line for pertussis treatment under 1 month and chemoprophylaxis is offered to all household contacts regardless of vaccination because vaccine effectiveness wanes and infants are at highest mortality risk. (Source: Australian Immunisation Handbook)

options.text: Admit the infant for observation only; commence household antibiotics only if the older sibling tests positive on swab
options.rationale: Treating only the infant misses the household reservoir and waiting for sibling testing delays prophylaxis beyond the effective window of 21 days from index exposure. (Source: Australian Immunisation Handbook)

explanation.summary: Pertussis in infants under 6 months carries the highest mortality, often from apnoea or secondary pneumonia. Diagnosis is by nasopharyngeal PCR. Macrolides (azithromycin preferred under 1 month) reduce infectivity and shorten symptoms if started within 3 weeks of cough onset. Chemoprophylaxis with azithromycin is offered to all close household contacts regardless of vaccination, ideally within 14 to 21 days of first exposure, because waning vaccine immunity allows transmission even in vaccinated family members. Notification to public health is mandatory.
key_points: Pertussis PCR diagnosis; azithromycin first line under 1 month
All household contacts get azithromycin prophylaxis regardless of vaccination
Treatment within 21 days of cough reduces transmission
Notifiable disease; public health unit contact required
explanation.pearls: Azithromycin in neonates carries a small risk of infantile hypertrophic pyloric stenosis; counsel parents about projectile vomiting at 3 to 5 weeks of life.

stem: You are the paediatric ED registrar reviewing a 9-month-old boy with 12 hours of episodic inconsolable crying, drawing his legs up, and now passing a small amount of dark stool with mucus per rectum. He has had two episodes of bile-stained vomit. Between attacks he appears lethargic but is now pale and clammy. He is well perfused but the abdomen feels fullness in the right upper quadrant. There has been no recent viral illness.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.0, HR 168, RR 40, SpO2 99% RA, BP 92/58, capillary refill 2 s
data_table.Examination: Lethargic between paroxysms; soft abdomen with palpable RUQ sausage-shaped mass; PR exam shows blood-stained mucus
data_table.Investigations: Abdominal ultrasound shows target sign at the hepatic flexure; FBC and UEC unremarkable; lactate 1.4

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Arrange same-admission air enema reduction by interventional radiology with paediatric surgical team in attendance
options.rationale: Confirmed intussusception in a stable child without peritonitis or perforation is reduced by air or contrast enema with surgical standby; success rate is around 80 to 95 percent. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)

options.text: Commence empirical IV ceftriaxone and metronidazole; observe for 6 hours before deciding on intervention
options.rationale: Antibiotics alone do not reduce intussusception; delaying enema risks bowel ischaemia, perforation and the need for surgical resection. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)

options.text: Proceed directly to laparotomy and manual reduction with potential resection; no enema attempt
options.rationale: Surgical reduction is reserved for failure of pneumatic or hydrostatic reduction, peritonitis, perforation or shock; first attempt should be radiological in stable children. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)

options.text: Discharge with safety-netting and arrange outpatient ultrasound in 24 hours if symptoms persist or worsen
options.rationale: Untreated intussusception progresses to ischaemia and perforation within hours; discharge of a confirmed case is unsafe. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)

options.text: Insert a nasogastric tube on free drainage and trial of bowel rest for 12 hours; reimage if no improvement
options.rationale: Bowel rest does not reduce intussusception; mechanical reduction by enema is required and delay increases bowel-resection risk. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)

explanation.summary: Intussusception typically presents in infants 3 months to 3 years with episodic crying, drawing up of legs, bile-stained vomiting and red-currant-jelly stool late in the course. Ultrasound is the diagnostic test of choice, showing the target or doughnut sign. Pneumatic or hydrostatic enema reduction succeeds in 80 to 95 percent of stable cases without peritonitis or perforation. Surgical management is reserved for failed reduction, signs of perforation, or shock. Recurrence after enema is 10 percent and usually occurs within 48 hours.
key_points: Ultrasound target sign confirms diagnosis
Air or contrast enema is first-line reduction in stable child
Surgery for failed enema, perforation or shock
Recurrence rate 10 percent, mostly within 48 hours
explanation.pearls: A pathological lead point (Meckel diverticulum, Peyer patch hypertrophy, lymphoma) is more likely in children over 2 years or with recurrence and warrants further imaging.

stem: You are the paediatric registrar reviewing a 4-month-old girl admitted 3 days ago with her first febrile urinary tract infection. The catheter specimen grew Escherichia coli sensitive to her current IV antibiotic. She defervesced within 36 hours and is now feeding well, with normal observations. The bladder ultrasound performed during admission showed mild left renal pelvis dilatation but no hydroureter. The team is preparing her discharge plan and follow up.

data_table.Examination: Well, alert, feeding normally; abdomen soft, no masses
data_table.Investigations: Catheter urine: E. coli sensitive; bladder ultrasound: mild left pelvis dilatation, normal right; FBC and CRP normalised; UEC normal
data_table.Medications: IV gentamicin and amoxicillin during admission; switching to oral cefalexin for discharge

lead_in: What is the most appropriate further imaging plan?

options.text: Magnetic resonance urogram at 3 months to characterise the renal pelvis and exclude pelvi-ureteric junction obstruction
options.rationale: MR urography is a second-line modality used for selected complex anomalies, not the standard pathway after first febrile UTI with mild pelvis dilatation. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

options.text: Routine MCUG within 2 weeks of discharge for every infant with a first febrile UTI regardless of imaging or clinical course
options.rationale: Routine MCUG for all first febrile UTIs is no longer recommended due to invasiveness, radiation and limited clinical benefit; selective use is the current standard. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

options.text: DMSA scan at 4 to 6 months to assess for renal scarring; no MCUG unless recurrent UTI or abnormal DMSA
options.rationale: Australian and NICE-aligned guidance recommend DMSA at 4 to 6 months post first febrile UTI in infants under 3 years with ultrasound findings or atypical features; MCUG is reserved for recurrent UTI or abnormal DMSA. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

options.text: Repeat bladder ultrasound only at 6 months with no further radiological investigation regardless of clinical course
options.rationale: Repeat ultrasound alone misses cortical scarring and reflux which are the key prognostic findings after febrile UTI in this age group. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

options.text: Continuous prophylactic trimethoprim 2 mg/kg nocte for 12 months with no further imaging unless symptomatic
options.rationale: Prophylactic antibiotics are reserved for proven high-grade reflux or recurrent UTI; first-time UTI without proven reflux is not an indication for routine prophylaxis. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

explanation.summary: Australian first-febrile-UTI investigation in infants under 3 years uses a tiered pathway. Ultrasound during the acute admission identifies obstruction or major anomaly. DMSA at 4 to 6 months identifies cortical scarring. MCUG is reserved for recurrent UTI, abnormal DMSA, or atypical features (non-E. coli, septicaemia, poor response to 48 hours of treatment). Antibiotic prophylaxis is reserved for proven high-grade vesicoureteric reflux or recurrent UTI.
key_points: Acute admission: ultrasound for all under 3 years
DMSA at 4 to 6 months to assess scarring
MCUG reserved for recurrence, abnormal DMSA or atypical UTI
Antibiotic prophylaxis only for proven reflux or recurrence
explanation.pearls: An atypical UTI is defined as non-E. coli, septicaemia, raised creatinine, poor urine flow, abdominal mass or failure to respond to appropriate antibiotic within 48 hours.

stem: You are the GP working in a regional clinic when parents rush in carrying their 18-month-old boy who suddenly began choking on a peanut 2 minutes ago. The toddler is conscious, distressed, and making weak ineffective coughing sounds with audible stridor. He is making poor air entry on auscultation and his lips are starting to look dusky. He cannot vocalise. The nearest hospital with paediatric emergency capability is 25 minutes away by ambulance.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.5, HR 168, RR 50, SpO2 86% on room air, BP not yet checked
data_table.Examination: Conscious, distressed, making feeble coughs with stridor; reduced air entry bilaterally; dusky lips
data_table.Investigations: Clinical assessment; no time for imaging

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate action?

options.text: Perform 5 abdominal thrusts (Heimlich manoeuvre) immediately and call for senior assistance
options.rationale: Abdominal thrusts are reserved for children over 1 year as a second-line manoeuvre after back blows fail; primary use in a young toddler risks abdominal organ injury. (Source: ANZCOR Guideline 4)

options.text: Encourage the child to keep coughing and call an ambulance; provide oxygen by mask while waiting for transport
options.rationale: Encouragement only applies when the cough is effective and the child can vocalise; an ineffective cough with stridor and desaturation requires immediate airway clearance manoeuvres. (Source: ANZCOR Guideline 4)

options.text: Perform a blind finger sweep of the oropharynx to locate the foreign body before attempting back blows
options.rationale: Blind finger sweeps are contraindicated because they can push the object deeper or cause oedema; only remove if visible and easily graspable. (Source: ANZCOR Guideline 4)

options.text: Give 5 back blows between the scapulae with the child head-down, followed by 5 chest thrusts if obstruction persists
options.rationale: Conscious infant or toddler with ineffective cough and airway obstruction is managed by alternating 5 back blows and 5 chest thrusts; abdominal thrusts are not used under 1 year due to liver injury risk and are second-line in older children. (Source: ANZCOR Guideline 4)

options.text: Lay the child supine and commence chest compressions at 100 to 120 per minute until ambulance arrives
options.rationale: Cardiopulmonary resuscitation with compressions is started only after the child loses consciousness from obstruction; in a conscious child, airway-clearance manoeuvres are first. (Source: ANZCOR Guideline 4)

explanation.summary: ANZCOR guidance for foreign body airway obstruction depends on age and cough effectiveness. Effective cough (forceful, vocalising, conscious): encourage cough and observe. Ineffective cough (silent, stridor, cyanosis, decreasing consciousness) in conscious patient: 5 back blows then 5 chest thrusts in infants under 1 year; 5 back blows then 5 chest thrusts in children over 1 year, with abdominal thrusts as second-line in older children. Unconscious patient: start CPR with chest compressions and inspect the airway during ventilation. Blind finger sweeps are contraindicated.
key_points: Effective cough: encourage and observe
Ineffective cough conscious: 5 back blows then 5 chest thrusts
Abdominal thrusts not used under 1 year
Unconscious: commence CPR and inspect airway during ventilation
explanation.pearls: After successful expulsion, all children with peanut or similar high-risk aspiration require bronchoscopy assessment because residual fragments cause delayed pneumonitis.

stem: You are the GP registrar reviewing a 6-year-old boy whose parents are concerned about ongoing daytime soiling for 8 months. He passes small hard pellets every 3 to 4 days and has multiple episodes of liquid stool leakage into his underwear daily. He was toilet-trained at 3 years. He withholds and crosses his legs. His diet is poor in fibre. There is no blood, abdominal pain, weight loss or red-flag history. Abdominal examination is consistent with faecal loading.

data_table.Examination: Abdomen soft with palpable faecal mass in left iliac fossa and suprapubic region; perianal skin normal; no anal fissure; gait and lower limb neurology normal
data_table.Investigations: Clinical diagnosis; no imaging indicated; growth on 50th centile

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first step in management?

options.text: Commence twice daily oral docusate 12.5 mg without disimpaction and review fibre intake over the next 6 weeks
options.rationale: Docusate alone is insufficient and skipping disimpaction means overflow soiling continues; without clearing the impacted mass, laxative titration fails. (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation)

options.text: Disimpaction with high-dose oral macrogol (PEG 3350) over 3 to 7 days, followed by daily maintenance dosing and behavioural toilet program
options.rationale: Functional constipation with overflow soiling requires disimpaction first (high-dose macrogol over 3 to 7 days) followed by maintenance laxative plus a toilet-sitting program; treating the maintenance phase without disimpaction prolongs soiling. (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation)

options.text: Refer for anorectal manometry and rectal biopsy to exclude Hirschsprung disease before any laxative intervention
options.rationale: Hirschsprung disease typically presents with delayed meconium passage and failure to thrive; a toilet-trained 6-year-old with classic functional pattern does not require biopsy as a first step. (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation)

options.text: Recommend daily phosphate enemas for 14 days as primary therapy and review symptoms at the end of the course
options.rationale: Phosphate enemas carry risks of hyperphosphataemia and hypocalcaemia in children and are not first-line; oral macrogol is safer and equally effective for disimpaction. (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation)

options.text: Refer to paediatric psychology for behavioural management of encopresis without medication for the first 3 months
options.rationale: Behavioural therapy alone fails when an impacted rectal mass causes ongoing overflow; pharmacological disimpaction and maintenance are needed together with the behavioural program. (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation)

explanation.summary: Functional constipation with overflow soiling requires a two-phase approach: disimpaction followed by maintenance. Disimpaction uses high-dose oral macrogol (1 to 1.5 g/kg/day for 3 to 7 days) until clear stools; maintenance uses lower-dose daily macrogol titrated to one soft stool daily. A behavioural toilet-sitting program (after meals for 5 to 10 minutes) and dietary advice (fibre, fluids) accompany medication. Red flags warranting further workup include delayed meconium passage, failure to thrive, abdominal distension, abnormal neurological findings or sacral dimple.
key_points: Disimpaction before maintenance to stop overflow
Macrogol 1 to 1.5 g/kg/day for disimpaction, lower dose for maintenance
Behavioural toilet program after meals essential
Maintenance for at least 6 months and weaned slowly
explanation.pearls: Maintenance laxatives are often required for many months; relapse rates exceed 50 percent if weaned within 6 months of symptom resolution.

stem: You are the GP reviewing a 26-month-old boy at his routine health check. His parents report he has only 5 single words and does not combine them. He does not point to show interest, prefers to line up cars repetitively, and resists eye contact. He had no significant birth or perinatal history and his hearing screen at birth was normal. He has no regression of skills. His motor development is normal and he walks well. There is no family history of intellectual disability.

data_table.Examination: No dysmorphic features; growth 50th centile; gait and tone normal; no neurocutaneous stigmata; HEADSS not yet applicable

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Refer to speech pathology only and review in 6 months; defer paediatric review unless speech therapy alone fails to improve language
options.rationale: Isolated speech therapy without diagnostic assessment misses the broader social communication and restricted-repetitive behaviour phenotype and delays NDIS access. (Source: Australian National Guideline for Autism Assessment)
source_refs: Australian National Guideline for Autism Assessment

options.text: Reassure parents that boys develop language later and review at age 3 years if expressive language has not advanced over the next 9 months
options.rationale: Watchful waiting beyond 2 years for language delay with social communication concerns delays diagnosis and access to NDIS-funded early intervention which has the strongest evidence under 5 years of age. (Source: Australian National Guideline for Autism Assessment)
source_refs: Australian National Guideline for Autism Assessment

options.text: Commence a 6-week trial of a Mediterranean-style diet and omega-3 supplementation and review behaviour and language at the end of trial
options.rationale: Dietary interventions including omega-3 have no robust evidence for autism management and delay access to evidence-based behavioural therapy. (Source: Australian National Guideline for Autism Assessment)
source_refs: Australian National Guideline for Autism Assessment

options.text: Order karyotype and Fragile X testing as the first investigation step before any specialist therapy referral or further developmental assessment
options.rationale: Genetic testing has a role in autism workup but is not the gatekeeper; multidisciplinary assessment and early intervention should commence in parallel, not wait for results. (Source: Australian National Guideline for Autism Assessment)
source_refs: Australian National Guideline for Autism Assessment

options.text: Refer for audiology assessment and to a developmental paediatrician for multidisciplinary autism assessment; arrange early intervention referral
options.rationale: Persistent receptive and expressive language delay with social communication concerns warrants audiology to exclude hearing loss and parallel referral for multidisciplinary autism assessment plus early intervention; delaying referral wastes the critical intervention window. (Source: Australian National Guideline for Autism Assessment)
source_refs: Australian National Guideline for Autism Assessment

explanation.summary: Autism is a clinical diagnosis based on persistent deficits in social communication and restricted, repetitive patterns of behaviour or interests across multiple settings. Red flags by age 2 years include no two-word phrases, no joint attention, no pointing to show, lack of pretend play, lack of social reciprocity. Hearing assessment is mandatory to exclude hearing loss as a cause of language delay. Multidisciplinary diagnostic assessment, NDIS application and evidence-based early intervention (speech, OT, behavioural) should commence in parallel rather than sequentially.
key_points: Multidisciplinary assessment is gold standard for diagnosis
Hearing assessment mandatory to exclude hearing loss
Early intervention before age 5 has strongest evidence
NDIS funding from diagnosis supports intervention access
explanation.pearls: Under 9 years, NDIS access is available via the early childhood approach without a formal diagnosis if developmental delay is documented, accelerating intervention access.

sources.label: Australian National Guideline for Autism Assessment

stem: You are the obstetric registrar in the birth suite called urgently to a multiparous woman who delivered a 4200 g baby vaginally 15 minutes ago after a 3-hour labour. The placenta has been delivered and appears complete. She is now bleeding heavily with an estimated 900 mL blood loss collected in the drape. Her uterus feels poorly contracted on abdominal palpation. Her bladder is empty. There are no obvious lacerations on initial visual inspection of the perineum.

data_table.Examination: Uterus boggy and palpable at umbilicus; perineum intact on initial inspection; placenta delivered appears complete; bladder empty
data_table.Investigations: Group and screen on file; Hb on admission 118; FBC and coags pending; IV cannulae x2 in situ
data_table.Medications: IV oxytocin 5 units active management given at delivery

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate next step?

options.text: Transfer to theatre for examination under anaesthesia and manual exploration of the uterine cavity without further uterotonics
options.rationale: EUA is reserved for ongoing bleeding despite uterotonics, suspected retained products or trauma; first-line is fundal massage plus additional uterotonic for atony. (Source: RANZCOG: Primary PPH)

options.text: Insert a Bakri intrauterine balloon as the first uterotonic step without prior medical therapy attempts
options.rationale: Intrauterine balloon tamponade is escalated to when medical management with uterotonics fails to control atonic bleeding, not as the first step. (Source: RANZCOG: Primary PPH)

options.text: Commence bimanual uterine massage and give IV oxytocin 10 units bolus then 40 units in 1 L saline infusion over 4 hours
options.rationale: Uterine atony is the most common cause of primary PPH; immediate bimanual fundal massage plus additional oxytocin (bolus then infusion) is the first-line response while resuscitation and call for help proceed in parallel. (Source: RANZCOG: Primary PPH)

options.text: Administer intramuscular ergometrine 250 micrograms first-line before any further oxytocin or uterine massage
options.rationale: Ergometrine is a second-line uterotonic after additional oxytocin and is contraindicated in hypertension or pre-eclampsia; it is not the immediate next step. (Source: RANZCOG: Primary PPH)

options.text: Administer intravenous tranexamic acid 1 g over 10 minutes and observe for response before any uterotonic escalation
options.rationale: Tranexamic acid is given as adjunct within 3 hours of PPH onset, alongside not instead of mechanical and uterotonic management of atony. (Source: RANZCOG: Primary PPH)

explanation.summary: Primary postpartum haemorrhage is blood loss of 500 mL or more after vaginal delivery (or 1000 mL after caesarean) in the first 24 hours. The 4Ts framework guides cause: tone (atony, 70 percent), trauma, tissue (retained products), thrombin. Immediate management is parallel: call for help, ABC resuscitation, identify cause, treat. For atony: bimanual fundal massage, additional oxytocin (bolus then infusion), then ergometrine (if no hypertension), then carboprost or misoprostol, then mechanical (balloon, B-Lynch suture), then surgical (interventional radiology, hysterectomy). Tranexamic acid 1 g IV within 3 hours of onset reduces mortality.
key_points: 4Ts: Tone, Trauma, Tissue, Thrombin
Atony is most common cause; uterotonic ladder starts with oxytocin
Ergometrine contraindicated in hypertension/pre-eclampsia
Tranexamic acid 1 g IV within 3 hours reduces mortality
explanation.pearls: Misoprostol 800 micrograms rectally or sublingually is an alternative uterotonic when oxytocin infusion is delayed or refrigeration is unreliable in rural settings.

stem: You are the GP reviewing a 32-year-old primiparous breastfeeding woman 3 weeks postpartum. She presents with a 36-hour history of right breast pain, redness in the upper outer quadrant, fever and rigors. She is otherwise well. She is continuing to breastfeed but the affected breast is exquisitely tender during feeds and she is considering stopping breastfeeding on that side. Ultrasound is not immediately available in your clinic. There is no skin necrosis or lymphangitic spread above the breast.

data_table.Examination: Right upper outer quadrant: erythema 8 by 6 cm, warm, indurated, no fluctuance; nipple intact, no cracking; no axillary lymphadenopathy
data_table.Investigations: Clinical diagnosis; FBC and CRP pending; breast milk not cultured

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Cease breastfeeding from both breasts; bind the breasts and prescribe bromocriptine to suppress lactation
options.rationale: Stopping breastfeeding and suppressing lactation worsens mastitis by impairing drainage; continued effective milk removal is central to recovery. (Source: ABM Protocol 36: Mastitis)

options.text: Encourage continued breastfeeding plus oral di(cloxacillin) 500 mg every 6 hours for 5 days; review at 48 hours
options.rationale: Lactational mastitis with systemic features warrants oral antistaphylococcal antibiotics and continued effective milk drainage; review in 48 hours to detect abscess formation requiring drainage. (Source: ABM Protocol 36: Mastitis)

options.text: Refer urgently for needle aspiration of the inflamed area without antibiotic therapy pending culture results
options.rationale: Aspiration is reserved for fluctuant collections suggestive of abscess; in the absence of fluctuance, antibiotic and effective drainage are first-line. (Source: ABM Protocol 36: Mastitis)

options.text: Prescribe oral trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 160/800 mg twice daily for community MRSA cover empirically
options.rationale: MRSA cover is reserved for risk factors (recent hospitalisation, prior MRSA, treatment failure) or known regional prevalence; empirical first-line is antistaphylococcal penicillin. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Trial 48 hours of warm compresses and ibuprofen alone without antibiotics; reassess only if symptoms worsen
options.rationale: Systemic features (fever, rigors) and persistent over 24 hours mark progression beyond simple engorgement and warrant antibiotics from the outset. (Source: ABM Protocol 36: Mastitis)

explanation.summary: Lactational mastitis is most often caused by Staphylococcus aureus and presents with a wedge-shaped tender erythematous area, fever and systemic symptoms. Cornerstone of management is continued effective milk drainage (feeding on the affected side, hand expression or pumping) plus oral antistaphylococcal antibiotics (di(cloxacillin) or flucloxacillin 500 mg every 6 hours for 5 to 7 days) when systemic features are present. Abscess is suspected if a fluctuant area develops or if symptoms fail to improve within 48 to 72 hours; ultrasound and aspiration or surgical drainage are then indicated.
key_points: Continue breastfeeding on the affected side
Antistaphylococcal penicillin 5 to 7 days for systemic features
Ultrasound and drainage if no improvement in 48 to 72 hours
MRSA cover reserved for treatment failure or known risk
explanation.pearls: Inflammatory breast cancer mimics mastitis with peau d'orange skin changes; refer urgently if a non-resolving erythema persists in a woman not lactating or beyond standard treatment course.
